meta.title: Bulletin of the Grande Armée (Bautzen, 20–21 May 1813)

lexical_units.contextual_meaning: the battle's outcome is framed as a destiny to be decided
lexical_units.contrast_explanation: A battle does not literally possess destiny, but the outcome is construed as fate-like.
lexical_units.comparison_basis: Battle outcome is understood through destiny/fate.
lexical_units.basic_meaning_source: Littre / Dictionnaire de l'Academie francaise, historical sense
lexical_units.gloss_notes: French `destin` is not identical to English destiny; it may carry fate/outcome entailments.
lexical_units.review_notes: Source-language `destin` is retained; confidence moderate because phrase is conventional. Gallica OCR noise is visible in the local sentence; use the French span_text as reviewed source text and verify before quotation.

lexical_units.gloss_notes: French `morts` may be literal battlefield dead or memorialized sacrificial dead.
French `mort` may be literal battlefield death or part of sacrificial/glory framing.
French `mort` may be literal battlefield death or part of sacrificial/glory framing.
French `patrie` carries civic-sacral and national-affective force not captured by a single English equivalent.
